result:
The old house on the corner had a personality. It wasn’t haunted by ghosts, but by memories. The paint peeled like sunburned skin, and the porch swing creaked a lonely rhythm against the wind. I’d walk past it every evening on my way home from the train, and I always paused. There was a rosebush by the gate, untamed and thorny, that bloomed with a deep, defiant red. It was the only thing that seemed alive.

One night, a storm rolled in, sudden and violent. The next morning, the rosebush was in ruins, petals scattered like drops of blood on the wet sidewalk. I expected to feel a pang of loss, but instead, I felt a strange relief. It had fought hard, but sometimes, even beautiful things have to let go. The old house looked quieter that day, as if it had finally accepted its own silence.

I took a single petal home and pressed it into a book. It wasn't about preserving the rose, but about remembering the moment I stopped walking past and started noticing. The house still stands, empty and waiting. And every time I pass, I see the bare, gnarled roots of the bush, still holding the ground. I think it’s growing back.

I’d walk past it every evening on my way home from the train, and I always paused. There was a rosebush by the gate, untamed and thorny, that bloomed with a deep, defiant red. It was the only thing that seemed alive.

One night, a storm rolled in, sudden and violent. The next morning, the rosebush was in ruins, petals scattered like drops of blood on the wet sidewalk. I expected to feel a pang of loss, but instead, I felt a strange relief. It had fought hard, but sometimes, even beautiful things have to let go. The old house looked quieter that day, as if it had finally accepted its own silence.

I took a single petal home and pressed it into a book. It wasn't about preserving the rose, but about remembering the moment I stopped walking past and started noticing. The house still stands, empty and waiting. And every time I pass, I see the bare, gnarled roots of the bush, still holding the ground. I think it’s growing back.
